coredata.dc:title: Enhanced ferrate(VI) oxidation of organic pollutants through direct electron transfer

coredata.dc:description: Fe(VI) is a versatile agent for water purification, and various strategies have been developed to improve its pollutant removal efficiency. Herein, it was found that in addition to intermediate iron species [Fe(IV)/Fe(V)], direct electron transfer (DET) played a significant role in the abatement of organic pollutants in Fe(VI)/carbon quantum dots (CQDs) system. Around 86, 83, 73, 64, 52, 45 and 17% of BPA, DCF, SMX, 4-CP, phenol, p-HBA, and IBP (6 μM) could be oxidized by 30 μM of Fe(VI), whereas with the addition of CQDs (4 mg/L), the oxidation ratio of these pollutants increased to 98, 99, 80, 88, 87, 66 and 57%, respectively. The negative impact induced by solution pH and background constituents on Fe(VI) abatement of pollutants could be alleviated by CQDs, and CQDs acted as catalysts for mediating DET from organic pollutants to Fe(VI). Theoretical calculation revealed that iron species [Fe(VI)/Fe(V)/Fe(IV)] was responsible for the oxidation of 36% of phenol, while DET contributed to the oxidation of 64% of phenol in the Fe(VI)/CQDs system. Compared with iron species oxidation, the CQDs mediated DET from pollutants to Fe(VI) was more efficient for utilizing the oxidation capacity of Fe(VI). The DET mechanism presented in the study provides a prospective strategy for improving the pollution control potential of Fe(VI).

Experimental condition: [Phenol]0 = 6 μM, [Fe(VI)]0 = 5.94 mg/L, [CQDs] = 4 mg/L, T = 25 °C.

Experimental condition: [Phenol]0 = 6 μM, [Fe(VI)]0 = 5.94 mg/L, [CQDs] = 4 mg/L, T = 25 °C, pH=7.

(B) Open-circuit potential curves of the CQDs-GCE electrode; (C) Amperometric i-t curve measurements using CQDs-GCE as the working electrode. Experimental condition: [phenol]0 = 6 μM, [Fe(VI)]0 = 5.94 mg/L.

head.ce:title: Enhanced ferrate(VI) oxidation of organic pollutants through direct electron transfer

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fe(VI) is a versatile agent for water purification, and various strategies have been developed to improve its pollutant removal efficiency. Herein, it was found that in addition to intermediate iron species [Fe(IV)/Fe(V)], direct electron transfer (DET) played a significant role in the abatement of organic pollutants in Fe(VI)/carbon quantum dots (CQDs) system. Around 86, 83, 73, 64, 52, 45 and 17% of BPA, DCF, SMX, 4-CP, phenol, p-HBA, and IBP (6 μM) could be oxidized by 30 μM of Fe(VI), whereas with the addition of CQDs (4 mg/L), the oxidation ratio of these pollutants increased to 98, 99, 80, 88, 87, 66 and 57%, respectively. The negative impact induced by solution pH and background constituents on Fe(VI) abatement of pollutants could be alleviated by CQDs, and CQDs acted as catalysts for mediating DET from organic pollutants to Fe(VI). Theoretical calculation revealed that iron species [Fe(VI)/Fe(V)/Fe(IV)] was responsible for the oxidation of 36% of phenol, while DET contributed to the oxidation of 64% of phenol in the Fe(VI)/CQDs system. Compared with iron species oxidation, the CQDs mediated DET from pollutants to Fe(VI) was more efficient for utilizing the oxidation capacity of Fe(VI). The DET mechanism presented in the study provides a prospective strategy for improving the pollution control potential of Fe(VI).

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ferrate [Fe(VI)] is effective for the abatement of pollutants due to the oxidation capacity of high valence iron species and the adsorption capacity of in situ formed ferric(hydro) oxides (Huang et al., 2018; Prucek et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2021b). Previous investigations have shown that the intermediate iron species [Fe(IV)/Fe(V)] formed during the reduction of Fe(VI) exhibit higher reactivity towards certain organic pollutants than Fe(VI) (Virender and Sharma, 1991; Wang et al., 2021a). As a result, strategies include adding acid [e.g., HCl, peracetic acid] (Manoli et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2022), metal ions [e.g., Mn(II), Fe(III), Co(II)] (Jiang et al., 2015; Prucek et al., 2015; Zhao et al., 2023), reducing agents (e.g., SO3 2−, S2O3 2−, NH2OH) (Feng et al., 2018), carbonaceous materials (e.g., carbon nanotubes, biochar, and hydrochar) (Pan et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2019; Tian et al., 2020) have been developed to active Fe(VI) and promote the formation of intermediate iron species for pollutant control. However, despite their high reactivity towards pollutants, intermediate iron species are also reactive with water and background constituents such as dissolved organic matter, H2PO4 −, HCO3 − (Wang et al., 2021b). The decomposition or competitive consumption of intermediate iron species in aqueous environment negatively impacts their potential for pollutant abatement.

The direct electron transfer (DET) process has been found to participate in the abatement of pollutants, in addition to the reaction of pollutants with oxidative species [•OH, SO4 •−, O2 •−, 1O2, Fe(IV), Fe(V), Mn(III), Mn(V), Mn(VI), etc.] (Ren et al., 2022; Shao et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2022a). In this process, the oxidant complexed with the catalyst forms an activated complex and directly transfer electrons form pollutants to the oxidant. The catalyst acts as the electron shuttle and may improve the utilization of the oxidation capacity of the oxidant (Duan et al., 2018a; Huang and Zhang, 2019; Lee et al., 2016). Investigations showed that the DET pathway has subsequent advantages in water purification. (1) The DET process could resist the negative impact of background constituents (such as natural organic matters and inorganic anions) on the abatement of target pollutants. In a DET-mediated reduced graphene oxide/peracetic acid oxidation system, humic acid, Cl− and HCO3 − had a small influence on the removal of sulfamethoxazole (SMX). The SMX removal ratio declined by only 7.9% when the concentration of humic acid increased from 0 to 10 mg/L and Cl− and HCO3 − had negligible effects on SMX oxidation with the dosages ranging from 0 to 1 mM. Due to the merit of DET, the removal ratio of SMX was enhanced by approximately 95% (Kong et al., 2022). (2) The DET pathway showed resilience over a wide range of pH and maintained the oxidation efficiency. For example, it was evidenced that the activation of persulfate by activated carbon was a non-radical process and p-chloroaniline removal was not significantly influenced by pH ranging from 3 to 9 (Yao et al., 2019). (3) The DET pathway had high selectivity toward electron-rich organic pollutants. For instance, the carbonized polypyrrole/peroxymonosulfate system can remove all phenolic compounds to a large degree except p-nitrophenol, while benzoic acid and its derivatives display negligible degradation except for p-hydroxybenzoic acid (Hu et al., 2017). (4) The DET pathway can avoid radical self-quenching or autodecomposition of intermediate metal species during the reaction. In sulfate radicals based oxidation, the too fast generation of radicals can lead to self-quenching themselves. The degradation effect of phenanthrene only increased by 10% when the concentration of persulfates increased 5 times (Yue et al., 2023). Similarly, Fe(IV) and Fe(V) species are reckoned as reactive species in Fe(VI) oxidation process, yet their lifespan is too short to be well harnessed in the aqueous environment (Shao et al., 2022). Nevertheless, the DET regime can overcome these shortages comparatively. The organic pollutants could be degraded directly through an electron transfer process, rather than generating intermediate Fe species first and then oxidizing pollutants. (5) The DET pathway could fully utilize the oxidized capacity of oxidant, prominently reducing chemical inputs and being more cost-effective. For instance, in a DET-dominated persulfate oxidation process, the reaction had a high peroxide utilization efficiency with low stoichiometry (2:1) of consumed persulfate (peroxymonosulfate) and target contaminants (phenol) (Ren et al., 2020). Considering the complicated constitutions in real water matrix, improving the contribution of DET pathway is a promising strategy for the oxidative treatment of pollutants.

Carbonaceous materials possess an electron-rich culture, versatile functional groups and defective sites, which endow them with good electronic conductivity and tunable structures (Duan et al., 2018b). Also, on account of their adsorbability, carbonaceous materials can easily combine oxidants and target pollutants. These features benefit carbocatalysts to transfer electrons from pollutants to oxidants (Ren et al., 2022). Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new-fangled type of nanomaterial. They are made up of unique, quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles with dimensions less than 10 nm (Liu et al., 2016). Their special electrical and optical properties of sp2 hybridization construction make them different from other carbon-based nanostructured materials like carbon nanotubes and graphene (Khan et al., 2022). Owing to their exceptional biocompatibility, low toxicity and remarkable stability, CQDs have garnered attention in the photocatalysis and electrocatalysis field. Hence, carbonaceous materials can react as excellent green catalysts to induce the DET regime in advanced oxidation processes. However, the possible contribution of the DET mechanism on Fe(VI) oxidation is ambiguous. If the DET pathway participates in Fe(VI) oxidation process, the utilization efficiency of Fe(VI) and intermediate iron species may be improved, and the practical application of Fe(VI) for pollutants abatement can be advanced.

Phenol, bisphenol A (BPA), diclofenac (DCF), sulfamethoxazole (SMX), 4-chlorophenol (4-CP), p-hydroxybenzoic acid (p-HBA), ibuprofen (IBP) and 2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonate) (ABTS) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Germany). All other chemicals were at least of analytical grade and used without further purification. All experimental solutions were prepared with ultrapure water produced by a Millipore system. A river water sample was collected from Songhua River at Harbin, Heilongjiang Province, China. The water sample was filtered through a glass fiber membrane (φ = 0.22 μm) and stored at 4 °C until use. K2FeO4 [Fe(VI)] was synthesized in the laboratory using previously described methods (Thompson et al., 1951). To prevent its oxidation by air, Fe(VI) solution was freshly prepared for each experiment by dissolving solid crystals of Fe(VI) in Milli-Q water. The solution was filtered through a hydrophilic acetate fiber membrane (φ = 0.22 μm) (Shanghai ANPEL, China) and the concentration of Fe(VI) was determined using a UV–Visible spectrophotometry at 510 nm. CQDs were prepared according to previous methods and more detailed information can be found in the Supporting Information TEXT S1 (Han et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oxidation of organic compounds by Fe(VI)/CQDs system
ce:section.ce:para: All experiments were conducted in 150 mL conical flasks with magnetic stirring under a water bath at 25 ± 1 °C in a dark environment to avoid exposure to light. Batch reactions were initiated by adding Fe(VI) solution into pH-buffered solutions containing an organic pollutant and CQDs. A volume of 2 mL of solution was sampled at different time intervals and quenched with an excess of hydroxylamine hydrochloride. Then, the sample was filtered through a 0.22 μm of glass fiber filter to determine the concentration of organic compounds by high-pressure liquid chromatography (HPLC). Variation of Fe(VI) concentrations was determined by an ABTS method reported by Lee et al. at a wavelength of 415 nm (Lee et al., 2005). All experiments were conducted at least in duplicate, and the average values and error bars were presented. The variation of the solution pH during the reaction process was monitored to less than 0.2 units.
Reaction kinetics of Fe(VI) or Fe(VI)/CQDs with organic pollutants were comparatively investigated under second-order conditions (detailed information is shown in the Supporting Information TEXT S2).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM1400, Japan) analysis was used to explore the surface configuration of CQDs. To examine the variation of surface functional groups and the chemical state of C, O on CQDs before and after the reaction process, samples were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Spectrum 100, PerkinElmer, Waltham, MA) and X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi, Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA). The reactive oxygen species were examined with electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR, EMX-8/2.7, Bruker, Germany) at room temperature. The EPR signals of •OH, O2 •− and 1O2 were identified by using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrrolidine N-oxide (DMPO) and 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-4-piperidinol (TEMP) spin trap agents, respectively.

The concentration of phenol was determined using high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC, Waters 2995) equipped with a Symmetry C18 column (4.6 × 150 mm, 5 μM of particle size, Waters) and a photodiode array detector (PDAD, Waters 2998). The mobile phase consisted of methanol (phase A) and 0.1% acetic acid (phase B) with a ratio of 40:60 (v:v) for phenol at a flow rate of 1 mL/min. The methods used to analyze other organic pollutants were outlined in TEXT S3. The pH measurements were conducted with a Leici pH-meter (PHS-3C, Shanghai), which was calibrated using standard solutions. Electrochemical measurements were performed in a three-electrode electrochemical cell equipped with an Autolab PGSTAT302N analyzer (Metrohm, Switzerland). The saturated Ag/AgCl electrode and platinum electrode served as the reference electrode and counter electrode, respectively. The working electrode was prepared by coating CQDs on a glassy carbon electrode (GCE).

To comprehend the validity of the coexistence of two working mechanisms in the Fe(VI)/CQDs system, density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed using modules. The specific approaches are elucidated in the Supporting Information TEXT S4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CQDs on Fe(VI) oxidation of organic pollutants
ce:section.ce:para: Seven kinds of commonly identified micropollutants, including BPA, DCF, SMX, 4-CP, phenol, p-HBA, and IBP, were comparatively studied for their efficacy in pollution control with Fe(VI) and Fe(VI)/CQDs. In the reaction with Fe(VI) (30 μM, 5.94 mg/L), around 86% of BPA, 83% of DCF, 73% of SMX, 64% of 4-CP, 52% of phenol, 45% of p-HBA, and 17% of IBP (6 μM) were oxidized, respectively (Fig. 1 A). This result was consistent with previous investigations that have shown the reactivity of Fe(VI) with these organic pollutants (Han et al., 2015). When 4 mg/L of CQDs were added into the polluted water, less than 2% of these organic pollutants were abated (Fig. 1A). The effect of CQDs on the oxidation or adsorption of these pollutants was inferior.

For the combination of Fe(VI) (30 μM, 5.94 mg/L) with CQDs (4 mg/L), the determined oxidation ratios of the above pollutants increased to 98% for BPA, 99% for DCF, 80% for SMX, 88% for 4-CP, 87% for phenol, 66% for p-HBA, and 57% for IBP (6 μM), respectively (Fig. 1A). Considering that less than 2% of the organics could be adsorbed by CQDs, the combination of Fe(VI) with CQDs effectively improved the oxidation capacity of Fe(VI) for pollutants abatement from 7% (for SMX) to 40% (for IBP) depending on the property of the target pollutant.

The second-order rate constant of Fe(VI) and Fe(VI)/CQDs with the pollutants was compared (Fig. 1B). For the Fe(VI) oxidation of BPA, DCF, SMX, 4-CP, phenol, p-HBA, and IBP at pH 7.0, the determined reaction rate constant was 539.87 M −1s−1, 187.50 M −1s−1, 276.28 M −1s−1, 166.33 M −1s−1, 74.80 M −1s−1, 113.43 M −1s−1, 160.37 M −1s−1, respectively. In Fe(VI)/CQDs system, the determined reaction rate constant was 1189.40 M −1s−1, 4270.70 M −1s−1, 285.78 M −1s−1, 890.32 M −1s−1, 478.83 M −1s−1, 300.90 M −1s−1, 291.43 M −1s−1, respectively (Fig. S1). The combination of Fe(VI) with CQDs improved the reaction rate constant from 1.1 times (for SMX) to 22.8 times (for DCF) approximately. Both the oxidation effect and reaction dynamic showed that CQDs improved the oxidation of pollutants by Fe(VI) across a wide range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH and background constituents on phenol oxidation in Fe(VI)/CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The oxidation capacity of Fe(VI) is highly pH dependent. The effect of pH on phenol oxidation in the Fe(VI)/CQDs system was investigated (Fig. 2 ). The results showed that Fe(VI)/CQDs exhibited superior oxidation efficiency compared to Fe(VI) at all pH ranges tested. At pH 5.0 and 6.0, Fe(VI)/CQDs system presented oxidation efficiencies of 73 and 81%, respectively, with 15% and 19% enhancement compared to Fe(VI), which oxidized 58% and 64% of phenol in 5 min, respectively. These results were in agreement with the reaction rate constants, where the k app values were 806.35 and 869.83 M −1s−1 for Fe(VI)/CQDs and 152.82 and 102.36 M −1s−1 for Fe(VI) at pH 5.0 and pH 6.0, respectively (detailed k app values are shown in Table S1). The high reactive activity of the two systems was attributed to the intense oxidative capacity of Fe(VI) at acidic pH, where the direct Fe(VI) oxidation process dominated the oxidation reaction.

At pH 7.0, Fe(VI)/CQDs system showed a 36% oxidation increasement in oxidation efficiency compared to Fe(VI). Although Fe(VI)/CQDs had mediocre intermediate Fe oxidative capacity in neutral conditions, the system exerted the highest oxidation efficiency. Alkaline conditions favor improving the stability of Fe(VI), but the reactivity of Fe(VI) would be negatively influenced in alkaline conditions. At pH 8.0, 46% of phenol could be oxidized within 10 min by Fe(VI). In comparison, 78% of phenol was oxidized by Fe(VI)/CQDs in 10 min. The determined k app values of Fe(VI)/CQDs at pH 8.0 was 201.5 M −1s−1, which was 4.5 times higher than Fe(VI) (44.33 M −1s−1). Likewise, although the Fe(VI) oxidation of phenol was inhibited at pH 9.0, Fe(VI)/CQDs system still displayed slight enhancement compared to Fe(VI), with determined reaction kinetics of 58.742 and 40.923 M −1s−1, respectively in Fe(VI)/CQDs and Fe(VI) groups. Fe(VI)/CQDs system presented pH resistance potential for phenol oxidation.

Background constituents would affect the abatement of pollutants by Fe(VI) in natural water. Hence, the efficiency of Fe(VI)/CQDs system under various conditions was examined. As shown in Fig. 3 , Cl− and SO4 2- showed negligible influence on phenol oxidation. However, phenol oxidation was suppressed with the existence of H2PO4 −. Specifically, the oxidation effectiveness of phenol decreased from 50% to 40% when the concentration of H2PO4 − increased from 1 mM to 5 mM, respectively. This decrease can be attributed to the complex interaction between H2PO4 − and iron species (Huang et al., 2018). Although the oxidation in Fe(VI)/CQDs was inhibited by H2PO4 −, its efficiency was comparable to that of Fe(VI) in water (with an oxidation ratio of 54%). Similarly, HCO3 − showed an inhibitory effect on phenol oxidation, resulting in a decrease in oxidation efficiency from 82% to 64% and 44% when the content of HCO3 − ranged from 1 mM to 3 mM and 5 mM, respectively. Nonetheless, even with the inhibitory effect of HCO3 − (at low concentrations of HCO3 −), Fe(VI)/CQDs still showed superior performance compared to Fe(VI) in pure water, with an oxidation efficiency of 54%.

Regarding cations, Ca2+ and Mg2+ exhibited slight inhibition on phenol oxidation, with the oxidation ratio decreasing from 72% to 65% and from 78% to 73% when the dosage increased from 1 mM to 5 mM, respectively. It was noticed that although the presence of cations reduced the oxidation efficiency, the oxidation effect remained superior to that of solo Fe(VI). Moreover, the effect of HA on the oxidation of phenol was explored. When 2 mM HA was added, it had a negligible effect with 86% oxidation of phenol. However, when the concentration of HA increased to 5 mM, phenol oxidation efficiency decreased to 60%. Further increasing the concentration of HA to 10 mg/L had no significant influence on the oxidation efficiency, with a constant 61% oxidation ratio. Basically, HA could consume the oxidant during the treatment processes, thus deteriorating the decontamination. Nevertheless, Fe(VI)/CQDs system maintained almost the same efficiency within 2 mg/L HA, which exerted excellent resilience. Even when 10 mg/L HA was added into the solution, Fe(VI)/CQDs system still exerted a 7% improvement in oxidation efficiency compared to the Fe(VI) system without HA. The above results indicated that Fe(VI)/CQDs performed better than Fe(VI) under various conditions. Considering the complexity of the water environment, the oxidation of phenol by Fe(VI)/CQDs in real water was also investigated. Albeit the effect of Fe(VI)/CQDs system was inhibited in real water with 61% phenol oxidation efficiency in 10 min, the system still showed a higher oxidation ratio than solo Fe(VI) discernibly, which only decontaminated 41% of phenol.

Combined with the above experiments, the results substantiated that the efficiency and effectiveness of the Fe(VI)/CQDs system were superior to that of Fe(VI) across a wide range of pH values and background constituents. The excellent performances of the Fe(VI)/CQDs system should be attributed to its unique mechanism, which set it apart from Fe(VI) oxidation. The Fe(VI)/CQDs system had great potential for practical application. The possible mechanism would be clarified in subsequent sections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Properties of CQDs before and after the reaction with Fe(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: The physicochemical properties of CQDs before and after the reaction with Fe(VI) were investigated. The prepared CQDs were highly dispersed, uniform in size, and possessed a nearly spherical shape (Fig. 4 A). The average size of the CQDs was 6.67 nm, which was consistent with the size distribution measured by the Malvern instrument. FTIR analysis suggested that both pristine and used CQDs had stretching vibrations at 1647 cm−1 (C=O), 1540 cm−1 [v(C=C), skeletal polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons], 1337 cm−1 (CO-C, indicating the existence of CQDs in a complex). The intensity of these peaks did not substantially change before and after the reaction with Fe(VI) (Fig. 4B). XPS analysis suggested that the C 1 s photoelectron spectrum of CQDs could be deconvoluted into C=C, CC, CH (284.3 and 285 eV), COH (286 eV), C=O (287.6 eV) and COOH (288.6 eV) (Fig. 4C). The XPS spectrum of O 1 s was also explored to confirm the presence of oxygen functional groups. The signals can be divided into CO (533.2 eV), and C=O (531.6 eV) (Fig. 4D). XPS results demonstrated that the content and category of functional groups of CQDs did not substantially change before and after the reaction with Fe(VI), which was in consistent with FTIR results that CQDs may act as the catalyst for enhancing Fe(VI) oxidation of organic pollutants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Contribution of different oxidants for pollutants oxidation

ce:section.ce:para: Intermediate iron species are known to play a critical role in the abatement of many pollutants when using Fe(VI) (Shao et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2022). Carbonaceous materials, including CNTs, graphene oxide, and biochar had been demonstrated to promote the formation of intermediate iron species in Fe(VI) oxidation process (Pan et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2019; Tian et al., 2020). In this study, the possible contribution of intermediate iron species to the abatement of pollutants in Fe(VI)/CQDs system was investigated.

Sulfoxide PMSO can be oxidized by high-valent iron [Fe(IV)/Fe(V)] into PMSO2 through oxygen transfer, which differs from the radical-based oxidation pathway (Li et al., 2018). Due to the low reaction rate of Fe(VI) with PMSO, PMSO can be used as a probe to study the formation of Fe(IV)/Fe(V) in Fe(VI) oxidation process (Shao et al., 2019). When 30 μM of Fe(VI) was reacted with 10 μM of PMSO at pH 7.0, 9.4 μM of PMSO was oxidized with the formation of 9.4 μM of PMSO2 (Fig. 5 A). However, when Fe(VI)/CQDs was reacted with 10 μM of PMSO, only 6.0 μM of PMSO was oxidized with the formation of 6.0 μM of PMSO2 (Fig. 5B). The oxidation ratio of PMSO in the Fe(VI)/CQDs system was 34% lower than that in the Fe(VI) system.

The second-order rate constant of PMSO with Fe(VI) and Fe(VI)/CQDs was found to be 218.11 and 71.96 M −1s−1, respectively. These results indicated that the formation of Fe(IV) and Fe(V) did not increase, but instead decreased in the Fe(VI)/CQDs system. Considering that the oxidation of PMSO to PMSO2 could reflect the formation of Fe(IV)/Fe(V), the inhibited formation of PMSO2 in the Fe(VI)/CQDs system indicated that the formation of Fe(IV)/Fe(V) had been suppressed. Additionally, in order to further confirm the inhibited generation of intermediate Fe species by CQDs, the effect of CQDs on Fe(II)/peroxydisulfate system was examined as Fe(IV)/Fe(V) are the reactive species in this system (Wang et al., 2018). As shown in Fig. S2, with the addition of 4 mg/L CQDs, the conversion ratio of PMSO to PMSO2 decreased from 70% to 50%. Therefore, CQDs inhibited the formation of intermediate iron species [Fe(IV)/Fe(V)] and they were not the only oxidative species responsible for the enhanced oxidation of organic pollutants in the Fe(VI)/CQDs system.

Besides intermediate iron species, other reactive species such as hydroxyl radicals (•OH), superoxide radicals (O2 •−), singlet oxygen (1O2) and photogenerated holes (h +) induced by CQDs may participate in the oxidation of organic contaminants (Chen et al., 2017; Han et al., 2020). Since the above experiment was carried out in the dark, the possible function of h + could be excluded. Radical scavenging experiments and EPR analysis were performed to explore the possible function of radicals in the Fe(VI)/CQDs system.

Methanol and tert‑butanol were utilized as quenching agents to examine the role of •OH. In the Fe(VI) oxidation of phenol, methanol and tert‑butanol showed negligible inhibiting effects, with 10% and 4% suppression, respectively, in the presence of 10 mM of methanol and tert‑butanol (Fig. 5C). For the oxidation of phenol in the Fe(VI)/CQDs system, 10 mM of methanol and tert‑butanol also exhibited slight effects, with approximately 10% suppression in methanol and tert‑butanol (Fig. 5D). These results implied that •OH was not the active oxidant for pollutants oxidation in the Fe(VI)/CQDs system.

To investigate the function of 1O2, furfuryl alcohol was used as the quenching agent. Around 50% of phenol could be oxidized by Fe(VI) within an hour. However, with the addition of 1 mM of furfuryl alcohol, the oxidation ratio decreased to 5%. In comparison, over 85% of phenol had been oxidized in the Fe(VI)/CQDs system, while the presence of furfuryl alcohol inhibited the oxidation ratio to 28% (Fig. 5E). It had been claimed that 1O2 was not the main reactive species in Fe(VI) oxidation process (Tian et al., 2020), and EPR spectroscopy analysis showed no signal of 1O2 in the Fe(VI) and Fe(VI)/CQDs system (Fig. S3). The inhibited oxidation of phenol by furfuryl alcohol in Fe(VI) or Fe(VI)/CQDs system may be ascribed to the competitive consumption of Fe(IV)/Fe(V)/Fe(VI) by furfuryl alcohol.

To verify the oxidation of furfuryl alcohol in Fe(VI) or Fe(VI)/CQDs systems, the decomposition of Fe(VI) in both systems was investigated (Fig. 5F). The ratio of residual Fe(VI) was found to be 6% after 40 min of reaction in both groups [Fe(VI) self-decomposition]. However, upon the addition of furfuryl alcohol, an accelerated consumption of Fe(VI) was observed in both groups. Almost no Fe(VI) existed after 1 min with the addition of furfuryl alcohol in both systems, thus confirming the reaction of Fe(IV)/Fe(V)/Fe(VI) with furfuryl alcohol. It should be noticed that even though the inhibition effect was observed with the same content of furfuryl alcohol, 28% of phenol was still oxidized in Fe(VI)/CQDs compared to Fe(VI) (Fig. 5E). Hence, other reactive species may be responsible for the enhanced oxidation of phenol in the Fe(VI)/CQDs system.

Apart from the radical scavenging experiment, EPR spectroscopy was also employed to detect the possible existence of •OH or O2 •− radicals using 5,5-dimethyl-pyrroline-oxide (DMPO) as a spin trap (Fig. S3). The absence of an obvious DMPOOH adduct peak (1:2:2:1) or DMPOO2 •− (1:1:1:1) in the Fe(VI)/CQDs system indicated that neither •OH nor O2 •− played an important role in this system. Albeit EPR spectrum signals of DMPOOH adduct were detected in the Fe(VI) system, the formation of false positive signals of •OH was ascribed to inverted spin trapping or the Forrester-Hepburn mechanism and DMPOOH adduct could be generated due to the oxidation of DMPO by Fe(V) and Fe(IV) (Jing and Chaplin, 2017; Shao et al., 2019). Similarly, when 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-4-piperidinol (TEMP) was added to identify the 1O2, no triplet EPR spectrum corresponding to the oxidized TMP by 1O2 was observed (Figure S3). Overall, the reactive species •OH, O2 •−, 1O2 and h + are not the main reason responsible for the enhanced oxidation of phenol in the Fe(VI)/CQDs system.

Based on the analysis conducted above, it can be concluded that intermediate iron species are involved in the abatement of organic pollutants but are not solely responsible for the complete oxidation of the pollutants. Previous investigations have reported that carbonaceous materials may play a dual role in carbocatalytic oxidation processes: (1) carbonaceous materials can act as reductants to react with oxidants for the generation of intermediate species (Sanchez-Polo et al., 2005; Tian et al., 2020). (2) carbonaceous materials would mediate electron transfer from contaminants to oxidants via the formation of a charge transfer complex, namely, direct electron transfer (DET) (Lee et al., 2016; Ren et al., 2019). In the Fe(VI)/CQDs system, CQDs may catalyze the oxidation of organic pollutants through the DET process.

Fe(VI) would react with water and be reduced into Fe(III). When 30 μM of Fe(VI) had been added into water, approximately 26% and 48% of Fe(VI) decayed within 5 and 10 min, respectively (Fig. 6 A). Interestingly, the addition of CQDs did not affect the decay of Fe(VI). However, the content of Fe(VI) decreased to 24% in 5 min and 10.9% in 10 min in Fe(VI) oxidation of phenol, while the content of Fe(VI) decreased to 6.5% in 5 min and 3% in 10 min in the oxidation of phenol with the addition of CQDs. The consumption of Fe(VI) in Fe(VI)/CQDs-phenol system was faster than that in Fe(VI)-phenol system. The rapid consumption of Fe(VI) may be responsible for the enhanced oxidation of phenol. Considering that the properties of CQDs did not substantially change before and after the reaction with Fe(VI) (Fig. 4B and C), CQDs may act as electron shuttles in Fe(VI) oxidation process by mediating DET from phenol to Fe(VI).

Chronopotentiometry analysis was carried out to probe the possible electron migration pathway in Fe(VI)/CQDs system. The CQDs electrode showed an open-circuit potential (OCP) of +0.84 V vs Ag/AgCl, which decreased to +0.80 V after phenol addition (Fig. 6B). This indicates that phenol as an electron-rich compound can donate electrons to CQDs. The OCP of the CQDs electrode slightly decreased to +0.79 V with 30 μM Fe(VI) addition, possibly due to the adsorption of Fe(VI) on the electrode surface. When phenol was added into the solution, the OCP sharply decreased to +0.50 V, suggesting that phenol was oxidized by sacrificing electrons to CQDs-Fe(VI) complex. OCP drop was also observed upon phenol addition to GCE/Fe(VI) system, which indicates that Fe(VI) was responsible for the electron transfer process. Considering that phenol could donate electrons to CQDs and the OCP drop in GCE/Fe(VI) was less obvious than that in the Fe(VI)/CQDs system, we can deduce that CQDs act as electron shuttles to boost electron migration from phenol to Fe(VI). The chronoamperometric result also provides direct evidence for mediated electron transfer process between phenol and CQDs-Fe(VI), reflected by the sharply changed current output with phenol addition (Fig. 6C). The chronopotentiometry analysis and chronoamperometric analysis of other pollutants were depicted in Figs. S4 and S5, respectively. The similar decrease trend in OCP and output current of CQDs-GCE electrode under diverse pollutants revealed the function of CQDs was consistent in Fe(VI) oxidation process. The results substantiated that CQDs could mediate the electron transfer process from pollutants to the CQDs-Fe(VI) in a wide range.

Hence, combined with the aforementioned results, it can be assumed that both Fe(IV)/Fe(V) species and CQDs mediated electron transfer process contributed to the oxidation of phenol in the Fe(VI)/CQDs system. It is worth noting that this carbon/Fe(VI) system is different from the carbon/persulfate system, where carbon-based catalysts serve as electron donators to oxidants for pollutant oxidation by the electron transfer pathway (Tian et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Theoretical calculation about the interaction of Fe(VI) with CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Theoretical calculation had been used for revealing the interaction among Fe(VI), CQDs and phenol. Key parameters, including bond length of Fe-O (lFe-O), Bader charge and binding energy were presented in Table S2. Prior to simulation, the structures of Fe(VI), CQDs and phenol were optimized, and the charge density of the relevant chemicals was depicted in Fig. S6.

Based on the calculation result, the binding energy of the Fe(VI)/CQDs configuration was −0.73 eV. This result suggested that the interaction between Fe(VI) and CQDs is energetically feasible. The negative binding energy indicates an affinity of CQDs toward Fe(VI) and substrates (Ashish Tripathi et al., 2015). The CQDs contain CO and C=O bonds (Fig. 4). The COOH groups at the edge of CQDs could act as a ligand for binding iron species with the formation of Fe(VI)/CQDs complex to conduct electron transfer processes (Zhang et al., 2022b). The affinity of Fe(VI) and CQDs enables the CQDs can act as a bridge for electron transfer during the phenol oxidation process.

The length of the Fe-O bond could reflect the reactivity of Fe(VI). In the ionized state, the length of the Fe-Oc1 bond (Fe combined with mono-coordinated O) was 1.47 Å, and the length of the Fe-Oc2 bond (Fe combined with dual-coordinated O) was 1.54 Å. In comparison, the length of Fe-Oc1 was 1.49 Å and the length of Fe-Oc2 was 1.57 Å in the Fe(VI)/CQDs complex. The elongation of the Fe-O bond indicated that Fe(VI) was more activated in the form of the Fe(VI)/CQDs complex.

Bader's theory of atoms in molecules can be used for charge analysis by defining the hardness of atoms and quantifying the cost for removing charge from an atom (Yang et al., 2020). According to Bader's analysis, the Bader Charge of the Fe-Oc1 bond and Fe-Oc2 bond in an ionized state was 0.25e and 0.11e, respectively. In comparison, the Bader Charge of the Fe-Oc1 bond and Fe-Oc2 bond in the Fe(VI)/CQDs complex was 0.24e and 0.09e, respectively. The decrease in Bader Charge in the Fe-O bond indicated that the Fe(VI)/CQDs complex was more active than Fe(VI). Additionally, the charge of OCQDs was calculated to be 0.53e in the Fe(VI)/CQDs system, which was higher than that of Fe-O. This further demonstrated that electrons could be transferred from CQDs to Fe(VI) during the oxidation process. The interaction between Fe(VI) and CQDs could improve the reactivity of Fe(VI) and allow CQDs to function as an electron shuttle to transfer electrons from organic pollutants to Fe(VI). This direct electron transfer process enabled the theoretical oxidation capacity of Fe(VI) [acquiring 3 electrons from the target pollutants through the reduction of Fe(VI) into Fe(III)] can be fully utilized, thus resulting in the improved oxidation of organic pollutants (Fig. 1). The schematic diagram was depicted in Figure S7.

As mentioned above, both CQDs mediated DET process (Fig. 6) and Fe(V)/Fe(IV) participated in the oxidation process (Fig. 5), which were functional for the oxidation of pollutants. The proposed mechanism was further studied by theoretical calculation (Fig. 7 ). The molecular orbital energy levels of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) for different chemicals had been calculated (Fig. S8). The value of HOMO and LUMO suggests the theoretical electron-donating and electron-withdrawing capacity of the relevant chemical. A higher HOMO value indicates a stronger electron-donating capacity, while a more negative LUMO value indicates an electron-withdrawing capacity. The energy gap between HOMO and LUMO suggests the electron transfer capacity of a substance. The HOMO of phenol (-1.29 eV) was more negative than the LUMO of CQDs (1.07 eV), and the HOMO of CQDs (-0.83 eV) was more negative than the LUMO of Fe(VI) (2.27 eV). These data suggest that the electron transfer from phenol to Fe(VI) via CQDs was energetically favorable. The computational results were in agreement with the above electrochemical results. For the intermediate Fe(IV) and Fe(V) mechanism, Fe(IV)/Fe(V) with a more positive LUMO (2.48 eV, 2.31 eV) can readily attack phenol and abstract electrons from the HOMO of phenol (-1.29 eV), which was also agreed well with the above sulfoxide probe experiments.

In light of the experimental and computational results, we speculated that the two mechanisms in the Fe(VI)/CQDs system are simultaneously functional for the abatement of pollutants. On one hand, Fe(VI) can be reduced to highly oxidative Fe(IV) and Fe(V) species, which has been acknowledged widely in Fe(VI) oxidation system. On the other hand, phenol can be adsorbed on the surface of CQDs via hydrogen bonds, and Fe(VI) would combine with CQDs through carboxylic groups to form a reactive complex (Zhang et al., 2022b). Electrons of phenol can be transferred to Fe(VI) via CQDs, with CQDs acting as electron shuttles for enhancing phenol oxidation. As such, the Fe(VI)/CQDs system undergoes a direct electron transfer pathway for phenol oxidation, which is first reported in Fe(VI) oxidation processes to the best of our knowledge.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Contribution of DET-Mediated oxidation for phenol abatement
ce:section.ce:para:
The DET-mediated oxidation process was identified, yet its contribution in Fe(VI)/CQDs system was unknown. The contribution of DET process for the oxidation of phenol in Fe(VI)/CQDs system had been explored. Based on above results and published investigations, 3 reaction pathways for phenol oxidation co-existing in Fe(VI)/CQDs system: (1) Fe(VI) oxidation of phenol; (2) Fe(V)/Fe(IV) oxidation of phenol, and (3) CQDs mediated direct electron transfer from phenol to iron species.

It had been found that phenol would be oxidized by Fe(VI) to phenoxyl radical with the formation of intermediate products quinone. Quinone would further react with Fe(V) (Hua Huang et al., 2001). The reaction chance of Fe(V)/Fe(IV) with phenol is inferior due to the high reaction rate of Fe(VI) with phenol (Rush and Bielski , 1995). A kinetic model was established according to the possible reaction pathways for phenol oxidation in Fe(VI)/CQDs system, with the ignoring of the reaction of Fe(V)/Fe(IV) with phenol (Eq. (2)). In terms of the above kinetic experiments, the kFe(VI) was calculated to be 74.798 M −1s−1 and the kobs was calculated to be 478.83 M −1s−1.

Based on above calculation, the contribution of DET for phenol oxidation was 64% around, and the contribution of Fe(VI) for phenol oxidation was 36% around. This result specifically uncovered that the mechanism of phenol oxidation was changed from Fe(VI)/Fe(V)/Fe(IV) oxidation in Fe(VI) system to DET dominated process in Fe(VI)/CQDs system. However, it should be noted that the above results were based on the reported reaction dynamic that Fe(VI) was reactive with phenol and the chance of Fe(V)/Fe(IV) to react with phenol was inferior. The contribution of Fe(VI), Fe(V)/Fe(IV), and DET for the oxidation of pollutants depends on the chemical property of the target pollutants.

Fe(VI) is a multi-functional water purification agent, and investigations particularly focused on stimulating the formation of Fe(IV)/Fe(V) for pollutants abatement due to their high reactivity. Herein, it has been found that besides stimulating Fe(IV)/Fe(V) formation, utilization of CQDs as electron shuttles to facilitate direct electron transfer from pollutants to Fe(VI) is also an effective method for enhancing the abatement of pollutants. The CQDs mediated Fe(VI) oxidation process showed a remarkable 10–40% improvement in oxidation, along with a 1.1–22.8 times enhancement in reaction kinetics. CQDs are capable of complexing with Fe(VI) rather than engaging in redox reactions similar to other carbonaceous materials in Fe(VI) system. Due to the merit of co-existing intermediate iron species and direct electron transfer mechanisms, the Fe(VI)/CQDs system possesses a number of advantages over Fe(VI) in terms of its ability to function efficiently over a wide range of pH values and complex water matrices. Considering that the preparation of CQDs is easy and cost-effective, the integration of Fe(VI) and CQDs is promising for the elimination of contaminants from polluted water.
